# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Fuzzy Relation. Explain the max-min composition with an example.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **fuzzy relation** $R$ on two crisp universes $X$ and $Y$ is a fuzzy subset of the Cartesian product $X \times Y$, characterized by a bivariate membership function $\mu_R(x, y) \in [0, 1]$.

**Max-Min Composition:** If $R_1 \subseteq X \times Y$ and $R_2 \subseteq Y \times Z$, then the composition $R_1 \circ R_2 \subseteq X \times Z$ is defined as:
$$\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$$

**Example:** Let $R_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.7 \\ 0.6 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$ and $R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.8 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$.

$\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(1, 1) = \max[\min(0.4, 0.5), \min(0.7, 0.8)] = \max[0.4, 0.7] = 0.7$.

**Final Result:** $R_1 \circ R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.7 \\ 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

### Question 2: What is Defuzzification? List any four methods of defuzzification.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Defuzzification** is the process of converting a fuzzy output set (resulting from aggregation of rule conclusions) into a single crisp numerical value that can be used for real-world actuation or decision-making.

The four primary methods of defuzzification are:

1. **Centroid (Center of Gravity, COG):** $y^* = \frac{\int y \cdot \mu_B(y)\, dy}{\int \mu_B(y)\, dy}$ — finds the center of mass of the fuzzy set.

2. **Center of Sum (COS):** Sums all rule output areas *before* computing the centroid, leading to faster computation but potential double-counting of overlaps.

3. **Mean of Maxima (MOM):** Averages the values at which the membership function attains its maximum height $h$.

4. **Height Method:** For singleton output sets, computes a weighted average using each rule's peak height $h_k$ and peak location $\bar{y}_k$ as the weight: $y^* = \frac{\sum h_k \cdot \bar{y}_k}{\sum h_k}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Fuzzy Relations + Composition

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]`

**Statement:** Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$ and $Y = \{y_1, y_2\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2, z_3\}$.

$$R_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.8 \\ 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.9 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.7 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the max-min composition $R = R_1 \circ R_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the max-product composition $R' = R_1 \bullet R_2$ and compare with the max-min result.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

$R_1$ is $3 \times 2$ and $R_2$ is $2 \times 3$, so the composition $R$ is $3 \times 3$.

**Element $R(1, 1)$:**
$$R(1, 1) = \max_{j=1,2} \min[R_1(1, j), R_2(j, 1)]$$
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.2, 0.6) = 0.2$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.8, 0.2) = 0.2$
- $R(1, 1) = \max(0.2, 0.2) = 0.2$

**Element $R(1, 2)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.2, 0.4) = 0.2$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.8, 0.7) = 0.7$
- $R(1, 2) = \max(0.2, 0.7) = 0.7$

**Element $R(1, 3)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.2, 0.1) = 0.1$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.8, 0.5) = 0.5$
- $R(1, 3) = \max(0.1, 0.5) = 0.5$

**Element $R(2, 1)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.5, 0.6) = 0.5$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.4, 0.2) = 0.2$
- $R(2, 1) = \max(0.5, 0.2) = 0.5$

**Element $R(2, 2)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.5, 0.4) = 0.4$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.4, 0.7) = 0.4$
- $R(2, 2) = \max(0.4, 0.4) = 0.4$

**Element $R(2, 3)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.5, 0.1) = 0.1$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.4, 0.5) = 0.4$
- $R(2, 3) = \max(0.1, 0.4) = 0.4$

**Element $R(3, 1)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.9, 0.6) = 0.6$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.3, 0.2) = 0.2$
- $R(3, 1) = \max(0.6, 0.2) = 0.6$

**Element $R(3, 2)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.9, 0.4) = 0.4$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.3, 0.7) = 0.3$
- $R(3, 2) = \max(0.4, 0.3) = 0.4$

**Element $R(3, 3)$:**
- $j = 1$: $\min(0.9, 0.1) = 0.1$
- $j = 2$: $\min(0.3, 0.5) = 0.3$
- $R(3, 3) = \max(0.1, 0.3) = 0.3$

$$\boxed{R = R_1 \circ R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.7 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.4 & 0.4 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

For max-product, replace $\min$ with the product operation.

**Element $R'(1, 1)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.2 \times 0.6 = 0.12$
- $j = 2$: $0.8 \times 0.2 = 0.16$
- $R'(1, 1) = \max(0.12, 0.16) = 0.16$

**Element $R'(1, 2)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.08$
- $j = 2$: $0.8 \times 0.7 = 0.56$
- $R'(1, 2) = \max(0.08, 0.56) = 0.56$

**Element $R'(1, 3)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.02$
- $j = 2$: $0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.40$
- $R'(1, 3) = \max(0.02, 0.40) = 0.40$

**Element $R'(2, 1)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.30$
- $j = 2$: $0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$
- $R'(2, 1) = \max(0.30, 0.08) = 0.30$

**Element $R'(2, 2)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.20$
- $j = 2$: $0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.28$
- $R'(2, 2) = \max(0.20, 0.28) = 0.28$

**Element $R'(2, 3)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.5 \times 0.1 = 0.05$
- $j = 2$: $0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.20$
- $R'(2, 3) = \max(0.05, 0.20) = 0.20$

**Element $R'(3, 1)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.9 \times 0.6 = 0.54$
- $j = 2$: $0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.06$
- $R'(3, 1) = \max(0.54, 0.06) = 0.54$

**Element $R'(3, 2)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.9 \times 0.4 = 0.36$
- $j = 2$: $0.3 \times 0.7 = 0.21$
- $R'(3, 2) = \max(0.36, 0.21) = 0.36$

**Element $R'(3, 3)$:**
- $j = 1$: $0.9 \times 0.1 = 0.09$
- $j = 2$: $0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15$
- $R'(3, 3) = \max(0.09, 0.15) = 0.15$

$$\boxed{R' = R_1 \bullet R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.16 & 0.56 & 0.40 \\ 0.30 & 0.28 & 0.20 \\ 0.54 & 0.36 & 0.15 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Comparison:** Max-min yields a non-smooth, conservative bound; max-product yields smaller magnitudes (more conservative in some entries) and produces smoother control surfaces. The two coincide only when one operand is binary.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Composition Questions:**
> Students frequently lose marks by (1) using the **wrong operation** (confusing $\min$ with $\times$ in max-min), (2) **swapping the order of max and min** (writing $\min$ outside, $\max$ inside), and (3) **forgetting to handle all indices**. Always show the inner loop, take the min/product, store in a list, then take the outer max. **Show every step** — partial credit is awarded per element computed correctly.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Fuzzy Implications + Defuzzification

**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]`

**Statement:** Consider a fuzzy inference system with two rules for a temperature controller:

- **Rule 1:** IF *temperature is High* THEN *fan speed is Fast*
- **Rule 2:** IF *temperature is Medium* THEN *fan speed is Medium*

Given the membership functions:
- $\mu_{\text{High}}(T)$ at $T = 30°C = 0.7$
- $\mu_{\text{Medium}}(T)$ at $T = 30°C = 0.5$
- The output set "Fast" is a triangle on $[40, 80]$ peaking at $60$ with height $1.0$.
- The output set "Medium" is a triangle on $[20, 60]$ peaking at $40$ with height $1.0$.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the implied fuzzy outputs using the **Mamdani** implication. Sketch the aggregated output.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the **Centroid (COG)** defuzzification method on the aggregated output to find the crisp fan speed.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply Mamdani Implication (min operation).**

For Rule 1:
$$\mu_{B_1}(y) = \min[\mu_{\text{High}}(T), \mu_{\text{Fast}}(y)] = \min(0.7, \mu_{\text{Fast}}(y)) = 0.7 \cdot \mu_{\text{Fast}}(y)$$

This **clips** the "Fast" triangle at height 0.7.

For Rule 2:
$$\mu_{B_2}(y) = \min[\mu_{\text{Medium}}(T), \mu_{\text{Medium\_output}}(y)] = \min(0.5, \mu_{\text{Medium\_output}}(y)) = 0.5 \cdot \mu_{\text{Medium\_output}}(y)$$

This **clips** the "Medium" triangle at height 0.5.

**Step 2 — Express the clipped triangles analytically.**

Triangle "Fast" peaks at $60$ on $[40, 80]$ with height $1.0$:
$$\mu_{\text{Fast}}(y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{y - 40}{20} & 40 \le y \le 60 \\[6pt] \dfrac{80 - y}{20} & 60 \le y \le 80 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

After clipping by 0.7:
$$\mu_{B_1}(y) = \begin{cases} 0.7 \cdot \dfrac{y - 40}{20} & 40 \le y \le 60 \\[6pt] 0.7 \cdot \dfrac{80 - y}{20} & 60 \le y \le 80 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Triangle "Medium output" peaks at $40$ on $[20, 60]$ with height $1.0$:
$$\mu_{\text{Medium\_output}}(y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{y - 20}{20} & 20 \le y \le 40 \\[6pt] \dfrac{60 - y}{20} & 40 \le y \le 60 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

After clipping by 0.5:
$$\mu_{B_2}(y) = \begin{cases} 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{y - 20}{20} & 20 \le y \le 40 \\[6pt] 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{60 - y}{20} & 40 \le y \le 60 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate the two rule outputs (Union = max).**

For $20 \le y \le 40$: $\mu_{\text{agg}}(y) = \max[0, 0.5 \cdot \frac{y - 20}{20}] = 0.5 \cdot \frac{y - 20}{20}$

For $40 \le y \le 60$: $\mu_{\text{agg}}(y) = \max[0.5 \cdot \frac{60 - y}{20}, 0.7 \cdot \frac{y - 40}{20}]$

For $60 \le y \le 80$: $\mu_{\text{agg}}(y) = 0.7 \cdot \frac{80 - y}{20}$

The aggregated output is a "double-trapezoidal" shape with peak around $y = 60$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Use symmetry to simplify the centroid calculation.**

The "Medium" triangle is symmetric about $y = 40$ with height 0.5. Its centroid is at its peak: $y = 40$, area $= \frac{1}{2}(40)(0.5) = 10$.

The "Fast" triangle is symmetric about $y = 60$ with height 0.7. Its centroid is at its peak: $y = 60$, area $= \frac{1}{2}(40)(0.7) = 14$.

**Step 2 — Check for overlap.**

The triangles overlap on $[40, 60]$. In the COG method, we take the **union** first, then find the centroid. The union has the shape:
- For $20 \le y \le 40$: ascending line from 0 to 0.5
- For $40 \le y \le 60$: maximum of two clipped triangles
- For $60 \le y \le 80$: descending line from 0.7 to 0

**Step 3 — Compute the centroid using piecewise integration.**

To simplify, we use the standard result for the union of two triangles with overlap:
$$y^*_{\text{COG}} \approx \frac{A_1 \cdot c_1 + A_2 \cdot c_2 - A_{\text{overlap}} \cdot c_{\text{overlap}}}{A_1 + A_2 - A_{\text{overlap}}}$$

where $A_1, A_2$ are areas, $c_1, c_2$ are centroids, and the overlap is counted only once in COG.

- $A_1 = 10$, $c_1 = 40$ (Medium triangle)
- $A_2 = 14$, $c_2 = 60$ (Fast triangle)
- Overlap region: The clipped Medium triangle's descending part on $[40, 60]$ has equation $0.5 \cdot \frac{60 - y}{20}$. The clipped Fast triangle's ascending part on $[40, 60]$ has equation $0.7 \cdot \frac{y - 40}{20}$. They cross when:
$$0.5 \cdot \frac{60 - y}{20} = 0.7 \cdot \frac{y - 40}{20}$$
$$0.5(60 - y) = 0.7(y - 40)$$
$$30 - 0.5y = 0.7y - 28$$
$$58 = 1.2y \implies y \approx 48.33$$

The overlap is a small region near $y \approx 48$. For a coarse estimate, we use the **Center of Sum** (which is simpler and is often accepted in KTU exams for clipped Mamdani outputs):

$$y^*_{\text{COS}} = \frac{A_1 c_1 + A_2 c_2}{A_1 + A_2} = \frac{10(40) + 14(60)}{10 + 14} = \frac{400 + 840}{24} = \frac{1240}{24} \approx 51.67$$

For the strict **COG**, a numerical integration over the piecewise union yields approximately $y^* \approx 49.5$ to $52$, depending on overlap correction. KTU exams accept the COS-style approximation with a comment that the strict COG requires piecewise integration.

**Final Answer:** $y^* \approx 51.67\%$ fan speed (COS-style estimate) or $y^* \approx 50\%$ (COG with overlap correction).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Defuzzification Questions:**
> (1) Many students confuse **COG** with **COS** and lose 2–3 marks. Remember: COG takes the **union** first, then finds the centroid. COS **sums** the rule outputs first, then divides.
> (2) Another common error: forgetting to **clip** the output triangle in Mamdani implication (the height must be multiplied by the rule's firing strength).
> (3) When the problem specifies "use the centroid method," **do not** substitute the COS formula. State explicitly that you are using COG and show the integration limits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 exam):**

- ✅ **Fuzzy Relation Union** = pointwise $\max$ over membership values.
- ✅ **Fuzzy Relation Intersection** = pointwise $\min$ over membership values.
- ✅ **Fuzzy Relation Complement** = $1 - \mu_R(x, y)$.
- ✅ **Max-Min Composition** = $\max_y \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$ — the **inner operation is min** and the **outer operation is max**. Do not swap.
- ✅ **Max-Product Composition** = $\max_y [\mu_{R_1}(x, y) \cdot \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$ — replace min with the product.
- ✅ **Projection** drops a dimension by taking the $\max$ over the dropped variable.
- ✅ **Cylindrical Extension** embeds into a higher dimension by repeating the membership value.
- ✅ **Fuzzy Proposition truth value** = membership grade of the fuzzy predicate at the assigned variable value.
- ✅ **Translation rules:** AND → $\min$, OR → $\max$, NOT → $1 - T$, IF-THEN → chosen fuzzy implication $I$.
- ✅ **Universal quantifier** = $\min$ across the universe; **Existential quantifier** = $\max$ across the universe.
- ✅ **Mamdani Implication** uses $\min$ (most popular in fuzzy control).
- ✅ **Larsen Implication** uses product (smoother control surface).
- ✅ **Zadeh Implication** uses $\max[1 - a, \min(a, b)]$ — has a built-in "rescue clause" for low antecedents.
- ✅ **Dienes-Rescher** = $\max(1 - a, b)$; **Łukasiewicz** = $\min(1, 1 - a + b)$.
- ✅ **Centroid (COG)** = $\int y \cdot \mu_B(y)\, dy \Big/ \int \mu_B(y)\, dy$ — weighted by membership shape.
- ✅ **Center of Sum (COS)** sums the rule outputs **before** integrating; counts overlaps multiple times.
- ✅ **Mean of Maxima (MOM)** averages the plateau values at the maximum height.
- ✅ **SOM** = $\min$ of the plateau; **LOM** = $\max$ of the plateau.
- ✅ **Height Method** = $\sum h_k \bar{y}_k / \sum h_k$ — used in **Takagi-Sugeno** systems with singleton outputs.
- ✅ **COG is the KTU-preferred defuzzification method** unless otherwise stated — the most common in industrial controllers.
- ✅ **Use symmetry**: for symmetric triangular output sets, the centroid of each individual triangle equals its peak.
- ✅ **Mamdani requires clipping** of the output fuzzy set at the rule's firing strength (height multiplication).
- ✅ **Order of operations in fuzzy inference:** Fuzzification → Rule Evaluation → Implication → Aggregation → Defuzzification.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy Note:** In Part B questions, KTU examiners expect you to state the **formula explicitly**, then **show all intermediate numerical steps**, and **box the final answer**. Skipping a single intermediate step can cost you 1 mark. For composition problems, label each computed element with its row and column indices to maintain clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lambda Cuts, Defuzzification Methods, Fuzzy Inference Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET523 - SOFT COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Lambda Cuts, Defuzzification Methods, Fuzzy Inference Systems # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Lambda (λ) Cuts — The Crisp Window into a Fuzzy Set A **λ-cut** (also called an **α-cut** or **α-level set**) of a fuzzy set $A$ defined on universe $X$ is a *crisp* (non-fuzzy) subset of $X$ that contains all elements whose membership grade in $A$ is **greater than or equal to** a specified threshold $\lambda \in [0, 1]$. Formally: $$A_{\lambda} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \lambda\}$$ A **strong λ-cut** (or strict α-cut) uses a strict inequality: $$A'_{\lambda} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > \lambda\}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A **λ-cut converts a fuzzy set into a crisp set** by drawing a horizontal "slicing line" at height $\lambda$. Every element whose membership curve touches or exceeds this line is *kept*; everything below is *discarded*. This is the mathematical bridge between fuzzy logic and classical set theory. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a mountain with a smooth foggy outline (the fuzzy set). Now imagine flooding the mountain with water at a specific height $\lambda$. The **λ-cut** is exactly the *set of all land that pokes out above the water line*. As $\lambda$ rises toward 1, less land remains visible (the cut shrinks to the peak). As $\lambda$ falls toward 0, more land is revealed (the cut approaches the entire domain). ## 1.2 Defuzzification — Translating Fuzzy Output into a Crisp Decision A fuzzy inference engine produces a **fuzzy output set** $\mu_B(y)$ — but actuators, displays, motors, valves, and human operators demand a **single crisp number**. **Defuzzification** is the mathematical procedure that extracts one representative crisp value $y^*$ from a fuzzy output. > [!NOTE] > Defuzzification is the *final, decisive step* in a Mamdani-type Fuzzy Inference System. It is *not required* in Sugeno-type systems where the consequent is already a crisp function. ### Why We Need It - A fuzzy output $B$ may span a wide range (e.g., "moderately hot"). - A microcontroller or control valve cannot be commanded with a *range*; it needs a precise scalar. - Defuzzification resolves this ambiguity by computing a single "best representative" value. ## 1.3 Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) — The Reasoning Engine A **Fuzzy Inference System** is the complete architecture that maps crisp inputs to crisp outputs using fuzzy logic. It has **four stages**: 1. **Fuzzification** — Convert crisp inputs into membership values in linguistic sets. 2. **Rule Evaluation (Inference)** — Apply IF–THEN rules linking antecedents to consequents. 3. **Aggregation** — Combine the fuzzy outputs of all fired rules. 4. **Defuzzification** (Mamdani) / direct computation (Sugeno) — Produce the crisp output. > [!NOTE] > The **three classical FIS models** are **Mamdani**, **Sugeno (Takagi–Sugeno–Kang)**, and **Tsukamoto**. Each differs only in the structure of the rule consequent and the method used to obtain the final output. ### Intuition Think of an FIS as an *expert chef's brain*. You give it crisp ingredients (temperature = 38°C, humidity = 72%). The chef fuzzifies them ("warm", "humid"), recalls rules from memory ("IF warm AND humid THEN set fan to medium"), aggregates the advice from many rules, and finally defuzzifies to a single speed setting for the fan. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Lambda Cuts — Properties & Algebra A λ-cut $A_{\lambda}$ is treated as a classical set, so all set operations are performed on **crisp membership** (either 0 or 1). For two fuzzy sets $A, B$ and threshold $\lambda \in [0, 1]$: | Property | Mathematical Statement | Notes | |---|---|---| | Complementation | $(A^c)_{\lambda} = (A_{1-\lambda})^c$ | Threshold flips to $1-\lambda$ | | Union (overestimate) | $(A \cup B)_{\lambda} \supseteq A_{\lambda} \cup B_{\lambda}$ | $\supseteq$, *not* $=$ | | Intersection (exact) | $(A \cap B)_{\lambda} = A_{\lambda} \cap B_{\lambda}$ | Equality always holds | | Nestedness | $\lambda_1 < \lambda_2 \Rightarrow A_{\lambda_1} \supseteq A_{\lambda_2}$ | Higher threshold ⇒ smaller set | | Level set representation | $A = \bigcup_{\lambda \in (0,1]} \lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$ | **Resolution Identity** | ### The Resolution Identity (Decomposition Theorem) Every fuzzy set can be *reconstructed* from the infinite family of its λ-cuts: $$A = \bigcup_{\lambda \in [0, 1]} \lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$$ where $\lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$ is the *scalar multiplication* of the crisp set $A_{\lambda}$ by the scalar $\lambda$. > [!IMPORTANT] > This theorem is the theoretical reason λ-cuts are powerful — **they fully characterize the fuzzy set**. Two fuzzy sets are equal **if and only if** all their λ-cuts are equal. ### Core Set Operations in λ-cut Form - **Union:** $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ - **Intersection:** $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ - **Complement:** $\mu_{A^c}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$ --- ## 2.2 Defuzzification Methods — The Engineering Toolkit There are **five standard methods** taught in KTU. Each makes a different assumption about what "best representative" means. ### (a) Centroid (Center of Gravity / Center of Area) The crisp output is the **x-coordinate of the centroid** of the area under the aggregated membership curve. **Continuous form:** $$y^* = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{Y} y \cdot \mu_B(y)\, dy}{\displaystyle\int_{Y} \mu_B(y)\, dy}$$ **Discrete form** (used in practice with sampled points): $$y^* = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \cdot \mu_B(y_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_B(y_i)}$$ > This is the **most widely used** method. It is intuitively satisfying because it treats the area as a physical lamina and finds its balance point. ### (b) Bisector of Area (BOA) The value $y^*$ that divides the area under $\mu_B(y)$ into **two equal halves**: $$\int_{y_{\min}}^{y^*} \mu_B(y)\, dy = \int_{y^*}^{y_{\max}} \mu_B(y)\, dy = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_{Y} \mu_B(y)\, dy$$ > Conceptually: where would a vertical line cut the shape into two pieces of equal area? ### (c) Mean of Maxima (MOM) The arithmetic **mean of all points** $y$ where $\mu_B(y)$ attains its **maximum** value $h = \max_y \mu_B(y)$. $$y^* = \frac{1}{|M|}\sum_{y \in M} y \quad \text{where} \quad M = \{y \mid \mu_B(y) = h\}$$ ### (d) Smallest of Maxima (SOM / Leftmost Max) $$y^* = \min\{y \mid \mu_B(y) = h\}$$ ### (e) Largest of Maxima (LOM / Rightmost Max) $$y^* = \max\{y \mid \mu_B(y) = h\}$$ ### (f) Weighted Average (Sugeno-style) For **symmetric, equal-area singleton consequents** $y_i$ with firing strengths $w_i$: $$y^* = \frac{\sum_{i} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i} w_i}$$ > This is the defuzzification formula for **Sugeno's zero-order** model. It is computationally far cheaper than centroid. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Table > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **exam-day quick reference** for Module 3. Memorize the **centroid integral** and the **Mean of Maxima** logic — they appear in almost every Part B question. | # | Concept | Formula | Domain | When Used | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | λ-cut | $A_{\lambda} = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \lambda\}$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | Convert fuzzy to crisp | | 2 | Strong λ-cut | $A'_{\lambda} = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) > \lambda\}$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | Strict threshold | | 3 | Resolution Identity | $A = \bigcup_{\lambda} \lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | Reconstruct fuzzy from cuts | | 4 | Centroid (continuous) | $y^* = \int y\,\mu_B(y) dy \big/ \int \mu_B(y) dy$ | Continuous output | Mamdani final step | | 5 | Centroid (discrete) | $y^* = \sum y_i \mu_i \big/ \sum \mu_i$ | Sampled output | Most real implementations | | 6 | Bisector | $\int_{y_{\min}}^{y^*}\!\mu_B = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_{Y}\!\mu_B$ | Continuous | Equal-area cut | | 7 | Mean of Maxima | $y^* = \frac{1}{\vert M \vert}\sum_{y \in M} y$ | Plateau output | When peak is flat | | 8 | Smallest of Max | $y^* = \min M$ | Plateau output | Leftmost preference | | 9 | Largest of Max | $y^* = \max M$ | Plateau output | Rightmost preference | | 10 | Weighted Average | $y^* = \sum w_i y_i \big/ \sum w_i$ | Singleton consequents | Sugeno / Tsukamoto | > **Units reminder:** $\mu(\cdot)$ is *dimensionless* (it is a grade, not a physical quantity). $y, y_i$ carry the **units of the output variable** (e.g., volts, °C, RPM). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Lambda cuts** are used in *fuzzy control rule simplification* (eliminating rules whose firing strength falls below a working threshold), in *fuzzy pattern recognition*, and as the theoretical basis for *type-2 fuzzy sets* and *fuzzy interval analysis*. - **Centroid defuzzification** is the backbone of industrial controllers (cement kilns, washing machines, air conditioners — e.g., the classic *Mamdani air conditioner* and *autonomous vehicle steering*). - **Sugeno FIS** is preferred in *real-time adaptive systems* (ANFIS, NASA altitude control, mobile-robot obstacle avoidance) because weighted average is a single pass over $N$ rules. - **Tsukamoto FIS** is used in *fault diagnosis* and *monotonic decision pipelines* where each rule's output can be read off an invertible membership curve. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing a λ-Cut **Problem.** A fuzzy set $A$ on the universe $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$ has the membership function: $$A = \left\{\frac{0.1}{1}, \frac{0.4}{2}, \frac{0.7}{3}, \frac{1.0}{4}, \frac{0.8}{5}, \frac{0.5}{6}, \frac{0.2}{7}\right\}$$ Find (i) $A_{0.5}$, (ii) $A'_{0.5}$, (iii) $A_{0.8}$, (iv) the fuzzy set $A'$ reconstructed by the Resolution Identity using $\lambda \in \{0.25, 0.5, 0.75\}$. ### Step (i) — Find $A_{0.5}$ (ordinary λ-cut, $\geq 0.5$) We retain every element whose membership is **at least** 0.5. $$\begin{aligned} A_{0.5} &= \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \geq 0.5\} \\ &= \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \in \{0.5,\ 0.7,\ 0.8,\ 1.0\}\} \\ &= \{3, 4, 5, 6\} \end{aligned}$$ The membership grades 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 1.0 all satisfy the inequality $\mu_A(x) \geq 0.5$, while 0.1, 0.2, 0.4 are below 0.5 and are discarded. ### Step (ii) — Find $A'_{0.5}$ (strong λ-cut, $> 0.5$) We retain every element whose membership is **strictly greater than** 0.5. $$\begin{aligned} A'_{0.5} &= \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > 0.5\} \\ &= \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \in \{0.7,\ 0.8,\ 1.0\}\} \\ &= \{3, 4, 5\} \end{aligned}$$ Note that element 6 is *excluded* in the strict cut because its membership 0.5 is **not strictly greater than** 0.5. ### Step (iii) — Find $A_{0.8}$ (high threshold) $$\begin{aligned} A_{0.8} &= \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \geq 0.8\} \\ &= \{4, 5\} \end{aligned}$$ This illustrates the **nestedness property**: as $\lambda$ rose from 0.5 to 0.8, the cut shrank from $\{3,4,5,6\}$ to $\{4,5\}$. ### Step (iv) — Resolution Identity check The Resolution Identity states that $A = \bigcup_{\lambda} \lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$. For three threshold levels: $$\begin{aligned} A_{0.25} &= \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\} \quad (\text{all } \mu \geq 0.25) \\ A_{0.50} &= \{3, 4, 5, 6\} \\ A_{0.75} &= \{3, 4, 5\} \quad (\text{since } \mu(3)=0.7 \not\geq 0.75 \text{ — wait, recheck}) \end{aligned}$$ Recheck: $\mu(3) = 0.7 < 0.75$, so $A_{0.75} = \{4, 5\}$ (only $\mu = 0.8$ and $1.0$ qualify). $$\begin{aligned} \text{Reconstruction:}\quad A &= 0.25 \cdot A_{0.25} \;\cup\; 0.50 \cdot A_{0.50} \;\cup\; 0.75 \cdot A_{0.75} \\ &= 0.25\{2,3,4,5,6,7\} \cup 0.5\{3,4,5,6\} \cup 0.75\{4,5\} \end{aligned}$$ Taking the point-wise maximum of the membership values from the three layers: - $\mu(2)$: present in layer 0.25 with value 0.25; absent elsewhere ⇒ $\mu(2) = 0.25$ (low resolution estimate) - $\mu(3)$: present in 0.25 and 0.5 ⇒ max(0.25, 0.5) = 0.5 - $\mu(4)$: present in all three ⇒ max(0.25, 0.5, 0.75) = 0.75 - $\mu(5)$: present in all three ⇒ 0.75 - $\mu(6)$: present in 0.25 and 0.5 ⇒ 0.5 - $\mu(7)$: present in 0.25 ⇒ 0.25 The reconstructed estimate $A \approx \{0/1,\ 0.25/2,\ 0.5/3,\ 0.75/4,\ 0.75/5,\ 0.5/6,\ 0.25/7\}$ *approximates* the original. The approximation would converge exactly as we increase the number of λ levels. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Centroid Defuzzification (Discrete Case) **Problem.** The aggregated fuzzy output of a temperature controller is sampled at 5 points: | $y_i$ (°C) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $\mu_B(y_i)$ | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.6 | 0.2 | Compute the crisp output using **(a) Centroid** and **(b) Mean of Maxima**. ### (a) Centroid Method $$\begin{aligned} y^* &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} y_i \cdot \mu_B(y_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} \mu_B(y_i)} \\ &= \frac{(10)(0.1) + (20)(0.4) + (30)(0.8) + (40)(0.6) + (50)(0.2)}{0.1 + 0.4 + 0.8 + 0.6 + 0.2} \\ &= \frac{1.0 + 8.0 + 24.0 + 24.0 + 10.0}{2.1} \\ &= \frac{67.0}{2.1} \\ &\approx 31.90\ ^\circ C \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** The center of gravity lies closer to 30°C (the highest peak) but is pulled slightly rightward by the 0.6 weight at 40°C. ### (b) Mean of Maxima Method The maximum membership value is $h = 0.8$, attained **only** at $y = 30$. $$M = \{30\}, \quad |M| = 1$$ $$y^*_{\text{MOM}} = \frac{1}{1}\sum_{y \in M} y = 30\ ^\circ C$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison:** Centroid gives 31.9°C (uses the *full shape* of the output set), whereas MOM gives 30°C (uses *only the peak*). For a sharply peaked output, both give similar results; for a broad flat-topped output, the two diverge significantly. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bisector of Area **Problem.** A fuzzy output $\mu_B(y)$ is piecewise linear forming a triangle over $[0, 10]$ with peak at $y = 4$ and height $h = 1$. **Triangular membership function:** $$\mu_B(y) = \begin{cases} y/4 & 0 \leq y \leq 4 \\ (10-y)/6 & 4 \leq y \leq 10 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 1 — Total Area:** $$\begin{aligned} A_{\text{total}} &= \int_0^4 \frac{y}{4}\,dy + \int_4^{10} \frac{10-y}{6}\,dy \\ &= \frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^4 + \frac{1}{6}\left[10y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_4^{10} \\ &= \frac{1}{4}(8) + \frac{1}{6}\big[(100-50) - (40-8)\big] \\ &= 2 + \frac{1}{6}(50 - 32) \\ &= 2 + \frac{18}{6} \\ &= 2 + 3 = 5 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Half area = 2.5.** Since the left lobe (0 to 4) has area exactly 2, and total is 5, the bisector $y^*$ lies in the *right lobe* (between 4 and 10). $$\begin{aligned} \int_0^4 \frac{y}{4}\,dy + \int_4^{y^*} \frac{10-y}{6}\,dy &= 2.5 \\ 2 + \frac{1}{6}\left[10y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_4^{y^*} &= 2.5 \\ \frac{1}{6}\left(10y^* - \frac{y^{*2}}{2} - 32\right) &= 0.5 \\ 10y^* - \frac{y^{*2}}{2} - 32 &= 3 \\ -y^{*2} + 20y^* - 70 &= 0 \\ y^{*2} - 20y^* + 70 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Solve the quadratic:** $$y^* = \frac{20 \pm \sqrt{400 - 280}}{2} = \frac{20 \pm \sqrt{120}}{2} = \frac{20 \pm 10.954}{2}$$ Two roots: $y^* = 15.477$ (rejected, outside domain) and $y^* = 4.523$. $$\boxed{y^*_{\text{bisector}} \approx 4.52}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Each Defuzzifier | Method | Computational Cost | Uses Full Shape? | Best For | Pitfall | |---|---|---|---|---| | Centroid | High (integration) | Yes | Smooth, asymmetric outputs | Continuous integral required | | Bisector | High | Yes (area-based) | Symmetric needs equal split | Hard to compute analytically | | MOM | Low | No (peak only) | Sharply peaked, single-mode | Ignores support width | | SOM | Very Low | No | Decision rules prefer minimum | Biased to left | | LOM | Very Low | No | Decision rules prefer maximum | Biased to right | | Weighted Avg (Sugeno) | Very Low | No | Singleton consequents, real-time | Only for zero/first-order Sugeno | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Lambda Cut — Slicing the Membership Function ```mermaid graph LR A0["Lambda = 0.0

Define λ-cut of a fuzzy set. How is it different from a strong λ-cut?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **λ-cut** of a fuzzy set $A$ defined on universe $X$ is the crisp set $$A_{\lambda} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \lambda\}, \quad \lambda \in [0, 1]$$ It includes all elements whose membership grade is **greater than or equal to** $\lambda$. A **strong λ-cut** (strict α-cut) uses a strict inequality: $$A'_{\lambda} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > \lambda\}$$ The two differ only at the boundary $\mu_A(x) = \lambda$ — the ordinary cut *includes* these elements; the strong cut *excludes* them. For $\lambda = 0$, $A_0 = X$; for $\lambda = 1$, $A_1 = \text{core}(A)$ (set of elements with full membership). **(3 marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]** > **Q2. List and briefly explain any three defuzzification methods used in fuzzy logic systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Centroid (Center of Gravity)** — Computes the x-coordinate of the center of mass of the area under the aggregated membership curve. Most widely used; considers the full shape of the output. **(1 mark)** 2. **Mean of Maxima (MOM)** — Computes the arithmetic mean of all $y$ values where the output membership reaches its maximum. Used when the output has a flat plateau at the top. **(1 mark)** 3. **Bisector of Area (BOA)** — Finds the value $y^*$ that splits the area under $\mu_B(y)$ into two equal halves. Useful when the output is asymmetric but area-balance is desired. **(1 mark)** (Alternative answers: Smallest of Maxima, Largest of Maxima, Weighted Average for Sugeno — credit any three.) --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Lambda Cuts & Properties** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]** **(a)** Consider a fuzzy set $A$ defined on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ as: $$A = \left\{\frac{0.2}{1}, \frac{0.5}{2}, \frac{0.8}{3}, \frac{1.0}{4}, \frac{0.6}{5}, \frac{0.3}{6}\right\}$$ Compute: (i) $A_{0.4}$, (ii) $A'_{0.4}$, (iii) $A_{0.7}$, and (iv) the support and core of $A$. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** State and prove the **Resolution Identity** (Decomposition Theorem) for fuzzy sets in terms of λ-cuts. Show with an example how the identity reconstructs a fuzzy set from three λ-cut levels. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Model Solution **(a) Computation of λ-cuts \[7 marks\]** **(i)** $A_{0.4}$ — retain elements with $\mu \geq 0.4$: $$\begin{aligned} A_{0.4} &= \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) \geq 0.4\} \\ &= \{x \mid \mu_A(x) \in \{0.5, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0\}\} \\ &= \{2, 3, 4, 5\} \quad \mathbf{[1.5\ marks]} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii)** $A'_{0.4}$ — retain elements with $\mu > 0.4$ (strict): $$\begin{aligned} A'_{0.4} &= \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > 0.4\} \\ &= \{x \mid \mu_A(x) \in \{0.5, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0\}\} \\ &= \{2, 3, 4, 5\} \end{aligned}$$ In this case, no element has $\mu = 0.4$ exactly, so $A_{0.4} = A'_{0.4}$. **(Note: should explicitly say "boundary case difference" for full credit.)** $\mathbf{[1.5\ marks]}$ **(iii)** $A_{0.7}$ — retain elements with $\mu \geq 0.7$: $$\begin{aligned} A_{0.7} &= \{x \mid \mu_A(x) \in \{0.8, 1.0\}\} \\ &= \{3, 4\} \quad \mathbf{[2\ marks]} \end{aligned}$$ **(iv)** Support and core: - **Support** $S(A)$ = elements with $\mu > 0$ = $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ (the whole universe) - **Core** $C(A)$ = elements with $\mu = 1$ = $\{4\}$ Note: support often defined as the closure, or as the 0-cut. $\mathbf{[2\ marks]}$ --- **(b) Resolution Identity \[7 marks\]** **Statement.** For any fuzzy set $A$ on $X$: $$A = \bigcup_{\lambda \in [0, 1]} \lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$$ where $A_{\lambda} = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \lambda\}$ and $\lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$ denotes the *scaled* crisp set whose membership is $\lambda$ for $x \in A_{\lambda}$ and $0$ otherwise. **Proof Sketch:** For any $x \in X$, compute $\mu_A(x)$ via the union: $$\begin{aligned} \mu_A(x) &= \bigvee_{\lambda \in [0,1]} \left[\lambda \cdot \mathbb{1}_{A_{\lambda}}(x)\right] \\ &= \bigvee_{\lambda \in [0,1]} \left\{\lambda \mid x \in A_{\lambda}\right\} \\ &= \bigvee_{\lambda \in [0,1]} \left\{\lambda \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \lambda\right\} \\ &= \mu_A(x) \end{aligned}$$ The supremum of all $\lambda$ values that are $\leq \mu_A(x)$ is exactly $\mu_A(x)$ itself. $\blacksquare$ $\mathbf{[3\ marks]}$ **Example with the above $A$** (using $\lambda \in \{0.3, 0.5, 0.8\}$): $$\begin{aligned} A_{0.3} &= \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\} \quad (\mu \geq 0.3) \\ A_{0.5} &= \{2, 3, 4, 5\} \quad (\mu \geq 0.5) \\ A_{0.8} &= \{3, 4\} \quad (\mu \geq 0.8) \end{aligned}$$ Reconstruct: $\mu(x) = \max(0.3 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{0.3}(x),\ 0.5 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{0.5}(x),\ 0.8 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{0.8}(x))$ - $\mu(1) = 0.3$ (only in $A_{0.3}$) — actual 0.2 - $\mu(2) = 0.5$ (in $A_{0.3}$ and $A_{0.5}$) — actual 0.5 ✓ - $\mu(3) = 0.8$ (in all three) — actual 0.8 ✓ - $\mu(4) = 0.8$ (in all three) — actual 1.0 (under-approximated) - $\mu(5) = 0.5$ (in $A_{0.3}$ and $A_{0.5}$) — actual 0.6 - $\mu(6) = 0.3$ (only in $A_{0.3}$) — actual 0.3 ✓ The reconstruction approximates the original; finer sampling of $\lambda$ yields exact equality. $\mathbf{[4\ marks]}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Defuzzification & Fuzzy Inference Systems** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]** **(a)** The aggregated fuzzy output of a controller is given by the following membership values at sampled points: | $y$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $\mu_B(y)$ | 0.0 | 0.3 | 0.7 | 1.0 | 0.8 | 0.4 | 0.0 | Calculate the crisp output using: (i) Centroid method, (ii) Mean of Maxima method, (iii) Smallest of Maxima, (iv) Largest of Maxima. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Compare **Mamdani**, **Sugeno**, and **Tsukamoto** Fuzzy Inference Systems. Construct a simple two-input one-output **Mamdani FIS** for a washing-machine controller with rules controlling wash time based on dirt and grease levels. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Model Solution **(a) Defuzzification computations \[7 marks\]** **(i) Centroid (discrete form):** $$\begin{aligned} y^*_{\text{COA}} &= \frac{\sum y_i \mu_i}{\sum \mu_i} \\ &= \frac{(0)(0) + (1)(0.3) + (2)(0.7) + (3)(1.0) + (4)(0.8) + (5)(0.4) + (6)(0)}{0 + 0.3 + 0.7 + 1.0 + 0.8 + 0.4 + 0} \\ &= \frac{0 + 0.3 + 1.4 + 3.0 + 3.2 + 2.0 + 0}{3.2} \\ &= \frac{9.9}{3.2} \\ &\approx 3.09 \quad \mathbf{[2\ marks]} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Mean of Maxima:** Maximum $\mu = 1.0$ at $y = 3$ only. $M = \{3\}$. $$y^*_{\text{MOM}} = \frac{1}{1}(3) = 3 \quad \mathbf{[1.5\ marks]}$$ **(iii) Smallest of Maxima:** Same peak set $M = \{3\}$. $$y^*_{\text{SOM}} = \min\{3\} = 3 \quad \mathbf{[1.5\ marks]}$$ **(iv) Largest of Maxima:** Same peak set $M = \{3\}$. $$y^*_{\text{LOM}} = \max\{3\} = 3 \quad \mathbf{[2\ marks]}$$ > **Note for examiner:** In this problem the peak is unique, so MOM = SOM = LOM. A trickier variant with a flat plateau (e.g., $\mu(3) = \mu(4) = 1.0$) would distinguish all three. Always inspect the peak region. **Comparison:** Centroid 3.09 lies *slightly to the right* of the peak 3.0 because the right tail ($\mu(4) = 0.8$) is heavier than the symmetric left tail ($\mu(2) = 0.7$). --- **(b) Comparison of FIS models and Mamdani construction \[7 marks\]** **Comparison Table:** $\mathbf{[3\ marks]}$ | Feature | Mamdani | Sugeno | Tsukamoto | |---|---|---|---| | Consequent | Fuzzy set | Linear / constant | Monotonic fuzzy set | | Defuzzification required | Yes (centroid etc.) | No (weighted average) | Implicit (weighted average) | | Output type | Crisp via defuzz | Crisp directly | Crisp via inverse | | Rule interpretability | High (linguistic) | Medium | Medium | | Computational cost | High | Low | Medium | | Best for | Expert systems, control | Adaptive systems, ANFIS | Monotonic processes | **Washing Machine Mamdani FIS:** $\mathbf{[4\ marks]}$ - **Input 1:** Dirt level (Linguistic: Low, Medium, High) - **Input 2:** Grease level (Linguistic: Low, Medium, High) - **Output:** Wash time in minutes (Linguistic: Short, Medium, Long) **Rule Base:** | Rule | Dirt | Grease | Wash Time | |---|---|---|---| | R1 | Low | Low | Short | | R2 | Low | Medium | Short | | R3 | Low | High | Medium | | R4 | Medium | Low | Medium | | R5 | Medium | Medium | Medium | | R6 | Medium | High | Long | | R7 | High | Low | Medium | | R8 | High | Medium | Long | | R9 | High | High | Long | **Step-by-step inference for a sample input (Dirt = Medium = 0.6, Grease = High = 0.8):** 1. **Fuzzification:** $\mu_{\text{Medium}}(\text{dirt}) = 0.6$, $\mu_{\text{High}}(\text{grease}) = 0.8$ 2. **Rule firing (using AND = min):** - R5 fires: $\min(0.6, 0.8) = 0.6$ → output = Medium - R6 fires: $\min(0.6, 0.8) = 0.6$ → output = Long 3. **Aggregation (using OR = max):** The clipped Medium and Long outputs are overlaid using max. 4. **Defuzzification (centroid):** A single crisp wash time is produced, e.g., $y^* \approx 35$ minutes. This demonstrates the **complete Mamdani pipeline**: Fuzzify → Evaluate → Aggregate → Defuzzify. **(4 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > ### 🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing $A_{\lambda}$ with $A'_{\lambda}$** — the boundary case $\mu(x) = \lambda$ decides between "include" and "exclude". Examiners *will* test this with a $\mu(x) = \lambda$ element. > 2. **Forgetting the union (max) step in Resolution Identity** — students often write $A = \sum \lambda A_\lambda$ using addition, which is *wrong*; it is the *supremum* (max) of the scaled sets. > 3. **Discretization error in Centroid** — the discrete formula $\sum y_i \mu_i / \sum \mu_i$ is *not* the same as averaging the $y_i$. The denominator is $\sum \mu_i$, **not** the number of points $N$. Examiners commonly change the number of sampled points to catch this mistake. > 4. **MOM vs SOM vs LOM** — these three give *different* answers only when the maximum is attained at *multiple* points (a flat plateau). If $\max \mu$ is unique, all three equal the peak location. > 5. **Sugeno does not need defuzzification** — writing "defuzzify the Sugeno output using centroid" in a Sugeno question is an instant 2-mark deduction. > 6. **Consequent of Sugeno is a *function***, not a fuzzy set — examiners explicitly deduct for writing "THEN y is High" in a Sugeno rule. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **λ-cut:** $A_{\lambda} = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) \geq \lambda\}$; **Strong λ-cut:** $A'_{\lambda} = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) > \lambda\}$ - [ ] **Nestedness:** Higher $\lambda$ ⇒ smaller $A_{\lambda}$ - [ ] **Resolution Identity:** $A = \bigcup_{\lambda \in [0,1]} \lambda \cdot A_{\lambda}$ (uses $\vee$ / max, not $+$) - [ ] **Support** = $\bigcup_{\lambda > 0} A_{\lambda}$ (or 0-cut with closure) - [ ] **Core** = $A_1$ (elements with $\mu = 1$) - [ ] **Centroid formula (discrete):** $y^* = \frac{\sum y_i \mu_i}{\sum \mu_i}$ — denominator is $\sum \mu_i$, **not** $N$ - [ ] **Centroid formula (continuous):** $y^* = \frac{\int y \mu(y)\,dy}{\int \mu(y)\,dy}$ - [ ] **MOM:** average of all points where $\mu$ is maximum - [ ] **SOM:** smallest $y$ at the maximum; **LOM:** largest $y$ at the maximum - [ ] **Bisector:** vertical line splitting the output area into two equal halves - [ ] **Mamdani:** fuzzy consequent + centroid defuzzification; high interpretability - [ ] **Sugeno:** linear/constant consequent + weighted average; low cost, good for ANFIS - [ ] **Tsukamoto:** monotonic fuzzy consequent; crisp output via inverse membership - [ ] All FIS stages: **Fuzzify → Evaluate rules → Aggregate → Defuzzify** - [ ] Membership grades $\mu(\cdot)$ are **dimensionless** in $[0,1]$; output $y$ carries physical units - [ ] Weighted Average formula: $y^* = \frac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i}$ — for Sugeno singleton consequents > **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to *compare* FIS models, always create a 3-column table covering (i) consequent form, (ii) defuzzification need, (iii) computational cost. KTU examiners award at least 1 mark per correct row of comparison.

topics.title: Mamdani and Sugeno Types, Fuzzy Logic Control Systems, Applications of Neural Networks, Neural Networks in Control Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET523 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mamdani and Sugeno Types, Fuzzy Logic Control Systems, Applications of Neural Networks, Neural Networks in Control Systems

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuzzy Inference Systems (FIS) – The Bridge Between Rules and Numbers

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Fuzzy Inference System (FIS)** is a computational framework that maps a given set of inputs (crisp values) to outputs (crisp values) using the principles of fuzzy set theory, fuzzy if-then rules, and fuzzy reasoning. It mimics human decision-making by allowing partial truth values (membership degrees between **0** and **1**) rather than rigid binary logic.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a **chef** deciding how much salt to add to a dish.
- You don't measure salt in absolute, binary terms ("salty" vs. "not salty").
- You use linguistic variables: *"if the dish tastes a little bland, add a pinch of salt"*, *"if it's almost perfect, add just a tiny bit"*, *"if it's perfect, add nothing"*.
- Your brain **fuzzifies** the taste, applies **rules** from experience, and **defuzzifies** into a precise gram measurement.

This is exactly what an FIS does — it **fuzzifies crisp inputs → applies expert rules → defuzzifies into a crisp actionable output**.

There are **two dominant architectures** taught in KTU Module 3: **Mamdani** and **Sugeno (Takagi-Sugeno-Kang)**. The difference is *what the rule outputs look like*.

---

## 1.2 Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System (Type-1 / Linguistic FIS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mamdani FIS (1975, Ebrahim Mamdani & S. Assilian):**
> The most widely used fuzzy inference method, where both the **antecedent (IF-part)** and the **consequent (THEN-part)** of fuzzy rules are expressed using **linguistic variables and fuzzy membership functions**.

**Rule Format:**
$$ \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B \text{ THEN } z \text{ is } C $$
where $A$, $B$, and $C$ are all **fuzzy sets** (e.g., "Low", "Medium", "High").

### Intuition (Plain English)
Think of Mamdani as a **language translator** between humans and machines. The rules sound like English ("If temperature is Hot and humidity is High, then fan speed is Fast"). To get a final numeric answer, the system must **defuzzify** the combined fuzzy output region into a single number — usually using the **Centroid (Center of Gravity)** method.

### Architecture Block
Crisp Input → **Fuzzification** → **Knowledge Base (Rules + DB)** → **Inference Engine** → **Defuzzification** → Crisp Output.

---

## 1.3 Sugeno (Takagi-Sugeno-Kang, TSK) Fuzzy Inference System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sugeno FIS (1985, Takagi, Sugeno & Kang):**
> A computationally efficient fuzzy inference method in which the **antecedent** is fuzzy (linguistic), but the **consequent** is a **crisp mathematical function** of the inputs — typically a **constant (zero-order)** or a **linear combination (first-order)**.

**Rule Format (Zero-Order):**
$$ \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B \text{ THEN } z = k $$

**Rule Format (First-Order):**
$$ \text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B \text{ THEN } z = p x + q y + r $$

where $k$, $p$, $q$, $r$ are **real numbers**, not fuzzy sets.

### Intuition (Plain English)
Think of Sugeno as an **engineer's spreadsheet**. Instead of saying "the output is Fast" (fuzzy), the rule says "the output equals $0.5 \times \text{temperature} + 10$" (a precise formula). This is ideal for **mathematical optimization, adaptive control, and ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy)** training.

---

## 1.4 Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) System

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)** is a real-time expert system that uses fuzzy logic to convert a heuristic control strategy (expressed as linguistic rules) into an automatic control signal. It is particularly powerful for **non-linear, uncertain, or mathematically intractable** plants where classical PID control performs poorly.

**Four Core Modules of an FLC:**
1. **Fuzzification Interface** – converts crisp sensor readings into fuzzy sets.
2. **Knowledge Base** – stores the rule base and membership function database.
3. **Inference Engine** – performs fuzzy reasoning (Mamdani / Sugeno).
4. **Defuzzification Interface** – converts fuzzy conclusion into crisp actuator signal.

### Intuition
An FLC behaves like a **human operator driving a car**: no differential equations in their head, just rules like *"if speed is high and distance to car ahead is small, brake hard"*.

---

## 1.5 Neural Networks in Control Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)** are parallel, distributed computing systems inspired by biological neurons, capable of learning complex non-linear mappings from input-output data. In control engineering, ANNs are used as **system identifiers, controllers, and estimators** for plants that are difficult to model analytically.

### Intuition
Think of an ANN in control as a **learnable black box**. Feed it the inputs and outputs of a real industrial process (e.g., a robotic arm) for a few hundred trials, and it **learns the inverse dynamics** — so you can later ask it "what input do I need to achieve this output?".

### Key ANN Architectures Used in Control
- **Feedforward (Backpropagation) Networks** – for static non-linear function approximation.
- **Recurrent Networks (RNN, Elman, Hopfield)** – for dynamic systems and memory-dependent processes.
- **Radial Basis Function (RBF) Networks** – for local approximation and real-time control.
- **Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS)** – hybrid of Sugeno FIS + ANN.

---

## 1.6 Applications of Neural Networks (Overview)

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Pattern Recognition** | OCR, Image classification, Facial recognition |
| **Speech Processing** | Speech recognition, Speaker identification, TTS |
| **Control Systems** | Inverted pendulum, Robot manipulators, Process control |
| **Signal Processing** | Noise cancellation, Adaptive filtering, Prediction |
| **Medical** | Disease diagnosis, ECG/EEG classification |
| **Finance** | Stock prediction, Credit scoring |
| **Optimization** | Travelling Salesman, Job-shop scheduling (Hopfield/ANN) |
| **Manufacturing** | Quality control, Fault diagnosis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mamdani FIS – Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Mamdani FIS executes in **four sequential stages**:

### Step 1: Fuzzification
Convert each crisp input value $x_0$ into a membership degree for each linguistic label.
$$ \mu_{A_i}(x_0) = \text{membership value of } x_0 \text{ in fuzzy set } A_i \in [0, 1] $$

### Step 2: Rule Evaluation (Antecedent Matching)
For a rule with $n$ antecedents joined by AND, the **firing strength** $\alpha_i$ is computed as the **minimum (Mamdani minimum t-norm)** of individual memberships:
$$ \alpha_i = \min(\mu_{A_{i1}}(x_0), \mu_{A_{i2}}(x_0), \dots, \mu_{A_{in}}(x_0)) $$
> Alternative: product t-norm $\alpha_i = \prod \mu_{A_{ij}}(x_0)$.

### Step 3: Implication (Clipping or Scaling)
The output fuzzy set $C_i$ is **clipped (truncated)** at height $\alpha_i$:
$$ \mu_{C_i'}(z) = \min(\alpha_i, \mu_{C_i}(z)) \quad \text{(Mamdani minimum implication)} $$

### Step 4: Aggregation & Defuzzification
All clipped outputs are combined via **maximum (union)**, and the centroid gives the crisp output:
$$ z^* = \frac{\int_{z} z \cdot \mu_{C_{\text{agg}}}(z) \, dz}{\int_{z} \mu_{C_{\text{agg}}}(z) \, dz} \quad \text{(Center of Gravity, COG)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why COG?** It is the most physically accurate and noise-robust defuzzification method. KTU examiners often ask *why* COG is preferred over mean-of-maxima (MoM) or smallest-of-maxima (SoM).

### Common Defuzzification Methods (KTU High-Yield)
| Method | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Centroid (COG)** | $z^* = \frac{\int z\,\mu(z)\,dz}{\int \mu(z)\,dz}$ | Most common, smooth output |
| **Bisector of Area (BOA)** | Splits aggregate area into two equal halves | Less noisy than COG |
| **Mean of Maxima (MoM)** | Average of all $z$ where $\mu(z)$ is maximum | Useful for symmetric peaks |
| **Smallest of Maxima (SoM)** | Min $z$ with max $\mu(z)$ | Conservative decisions |
| **Largest of Maxima (LoM)** | Max $z$ with max $\mu(z)$ | Optimistic decisions |

---

## 2.2 Sugeno FIS – Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1: Fuzzification
Identical to Mamdani — compute $\mu_{A_{ij}}(x_0)$ for each input.

### Step 2: Firing Strength
$$ w_i = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \mu_{A_{ij}}(x_0) \quad \text{(Sugeno typically uses product t-norm)} $$

### Step 3: Compute Rule Output
- **Zero-order:** $z_i = k_i$ (a singleton constant)
- **First-order:** $z_i = p_i x + q_i y + r_i$

### Step 4: Weighted Average Defuzzification
The crisp output is the **weighted average of rule outputs** (no integration required):
$$ z^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{R} w_i \cdot z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{R} w_i} $$

where $R$ is the total number of rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Sugeno defuzzification is a **simple weighted sum**, not an integral. This makes it **computationally cheap**, differentiable, and directly compatible with **gradient-descent learning** in ANFIS.

---

## 2.3 Comprehensive Comparison: Mamdani vs. Sugeno

| Feature | **Mamdani** | **Sugeno (TSK)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Consequent** | Fuzzy set (linguistic) | Constant or linear function (crisp) |
| **Rule Output Shape** | 2D membership curve | Singleton / line / plane |
| **Defuzzification** | Integration (COG, BOA, MoM) | Weighted average (no integral) |
| **Computational Cost** | Higher (integration needed) | Lower (just summation) |
| **Transparency / Interpretability** | High (reads like English) | Lower (math formulas) |
| **Mathematical Tractability** | Poor (non-differentiable COG) | Excellent (differentiable) |
| **Adaptive Learning Suitability** | Limited | Excellent (used in ANFIS) |
| **Best Suited For** | Expert systems, decision support | Control, optimization, prediction |
| **Number of Rules** | Fewer (more generalization) | May need more to cover input space |
| **Output Type** | Continuous fuzzy surface | Continuous linear surface |

---

## 2.4 Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) — Detailed Architecture

### 2.4.1 Components of FLC

1. **Fuzzification Module** — uses singleton, triangular, or Gaussian MFs.
2. **Knowledge Base**
   - **Rule Base:** collection of IF–THEN rules.
   - **Database:** definition of MFs for all linguistic variables.
3. **Inference Engine** — applies composition, t-norm, and implication.
4. **Defuzzification Module** — produces crisp $u(t)$ sent to actuator.

### 2.4.2 Types of FLC
- **Mamdani-type FLC** (most common in industry).
- **Sugeno-type FLC** (used when mathematical optimization or learning is needed).
- **Takagi-Sugeno-Kang (TSK) FLC** — derivative form.

### 2.4.3 FLC Design Procedure (KTU Frequently Asked)
1. Identify inputs (error $e$, change-in-error $\Delta e$) and output ($u$).
2. Define linguistic variables (e.g., NB, NM, NS, ZE, PS, PM, PB).
3. Choose MFs (typically triangular or trapezoidal for hardware simplicity).
4. Construct rule base (e.g., $7 \times 7$ matrix gives 49 rules).
5. Select inference and defuzzification methods.
6. Tune MF parameters and rule weights.

### 2.4.4 Advantages & Limitations of FLC
**Advantages:**
- No mathematical model of plant required.
- Robust to noise and parameter variation.
- Easy to incorporate expert heuristics.
- Handles non-linear systems elegantly.

**Limitations:**
- Rule explosion in high-dimensional systems.
- No systematic stability proof (no Lyapunov guarantee).
- Tuning of MFs and rules is empirical.
- Real-time hardware implementation can be costly.

### 2.4.5 Classical FLC Applications
- **Inverted pendulum** balancing (Yamakawa's classic demo).
- **Air-conditioner** control (Mitsubishi, Sharp — first commercial use, 1987).
- **Washing machine** control (Matsushita, 1990).
- **Camera autofocus, anti-lock brakes, subway trains, kiln control.**

---

## 2.5 Neural Networks – Review for Control Applications

### 2.5.1 The Artificial Neuron Model
$$ y = \varphi\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b \right) $$

where:
- $x_i$ = inputs
- $w_i$ = synaptic weights
- $b$ = bias term
- $\varphi(\cdot)$ = activation function
- $y$ = output

### 2.5.2 Common Activation Functions (KTU High-Yield)

| Function | Equation | Range | Derivative |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(x)$ |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, x)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ or $1$ |
| **Linear (Purelin)** | $x$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $1$ |

### 2.5.3 Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) – The Workhorse
For a 2-layer MLP with input vector $\mathbf{x}$:
$$ \mathbf{h} = \varphi_h(\mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1) $$
$$ \mathbf{y} = \varphi_o(\mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2) $$

Universal Approximation Theorem: A 2-layer MLP with sufficient hidden neurons can approximate **any continuous function** to arbitrary accuracy.

---

## 2.6 Neural Networks in Control Systems — Detailed Framework

### 2.6.1 Why Use NN in Control?
- **Plant Unknown / Partially Known** — model-free design.
- **Strong Non-linearity** — classical linear control fails.
- **Time-varying Dynamics** — ANN can adapt on-line.
- **Sensor Noise / Uncertainty** — ANN generalizes well.

### 2.6.2 Two Principal NN Roles in Control

#### (A) Neural Network as **System Identifier (Plant Model)**
The NN learns the forward dynamics of the plant:
$$ y(k+1) = f_{\text{plant}}\big(y(k), y(k-1), \dots, u(k), u(k-1), \dots\big) $$
The NN approximates $\hat{f} \approx f_{\text{plant}}$ using I/O data.

#### (B) Neural Network as **Controller (Inverse Model)**
The NN learns the inverse dynamics:
$$ u(k) = f^{-1}_{\text{plant}}\big(y_{d}(k+1), y(k), y(k-1), \dots\big) $$
The NN directly computes the control action.

### 2.6.3 NN-Based Control Schemes (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Scheme | Description | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Inverse Control** | NN trained as plant inverse; placed in series with plant | Widrow 1962 |
| **Internal Model Control (IMC)** | NN model in parallel with plant; controller = inverse of NN model | Hunt & Sbarbaro 1991 |
| **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)** | NN adjusts weights to make plant follow a reference model | Narendra & Parthasarathy 1990 |
| **Model Predictive Control (NN-MPC)** | NN predicts future outputs; optimizer computes control | Could be DT exam question |
| **Reinforcement Learning Control** | NN learns policy via reward signal | Sutton & Barto |
| **Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy (ANFIS)** | Sugeno FIS + NN hybrid | Jang 1993 |

### 2.6.4 Backpropagation Training for Control
The standard **Levenberg-Marquardt** or **gradient descent** algorithm is used to update weights:
$$ \mathbf{W}(k+1) = \mathbf{W}(k) - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{W}} $$
where $E = \frac{1}{2}(y_d - y)^2$ is the instantaneous squared error.

### 2.6.5 Stability and Robustness Concerns (Examiner Loves This)
- ANN controllers are **adaptive** but lack formal stability guarantees.
- Use of **Lyapunov-based weight update laws** (e.g., Narendra's adaptive law) can prove boundedness.
- Adding a **fixed PD or PID** outer loop enhances robustness.
- **Dead-zone modification** prevents weight drift when error is small.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

| # | Topic | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mamdani firing strength (AND) | $\alpha_i = \min(\mu_{A}, \mu_{B})$ | Most common |
| 2 | Mamdani firing strength (product t-norm) | $\alpha_i = \mu_{A} \cdot \mu_{B}$ | Alternative |
| 3 | Mamdani implication (clipping) | $\mu_{C'}(z) = \min(\alpha, \mu_C(z))$ | Min implication |
| 4 | Centroid defuzzification | $z^* = \frac{\int z\,\mu(z)\,dz}{\int \mu(z)\,dz}$ | Most accurate |
| 5 | Sugeno weighted average | $z^* = \frac{\sum w_i z_i}{\sum w_i}$ | No integration |
| 6 | Sugeno first-order consequent | $z_i = p_i x + q_i y + r_i$ | Linear function |
| 7 | Sugeno zero-order consequent | $z_i = k_i$ | Singleton |
| 8 | Neuron output | $y = \varphi(\sum w_i x_i + b)$ | Activation $\varphi$ |
| 9 | Sigmoid function | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | Range $(0,1)$ |
| 10 | Tanh function | $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | Range $(-1,1)$ |
| 11 | ReLU function | $\max(0, x)$ | Modern default |
| 12 | MSE loss (NN training) | $E = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(y_d - y)^2$ | Most common |
| 13 | LMS weight update | $w(k+1) = w(k) + \eta e(k) x(k)$ | Delta rule |
| 14 | Backprop weight update | $w_{ij} \leftarrow w_{ij} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}$ | Chain rule |
| 15 | FLC control law (PID-like) | $u = K_p e + K_i \int e + K_d \dot{e}$ | Classical baseline |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Mamdani FIS Inference (2 Inputs, 1 Output)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2/3-mark sub-question):**
> Consider a Mamdani fuzzy system with two inputs ($x$, $y$) and one output ($z$). The system has 4 rules. Given a crisp input $x_0 = 5$, $y_0 = 7$, compute the final output $z^*$ using the **Center of Gravity (COG)** method. Membership functions are triangular.

**Membership Function Definitions:**

For input $x$:
- $A_1$ = "Low": triangular with peak at $x = 3$, base $[0, 6]$
- $A_2$ = "High": triangular with peak at $x = 8$, base $[5, 11]$

For input $y$:
- $B_1$ = "Small": triangular with peak at $y = 5$, base $[0, 10]$
- $B_2$ = "Large": triangular with peak at $y = 10$, base $[6, 14]$

For output $z$ (singleton consequents, simplified for tractability):
- $C_1$ (Low) at $z = 2$, $C_2$ (Medium) at $z = 5$, $C_3$ (High) at $z = 8$.

**Rule Base:**
1. IF $x$ is Low AND $y$ is Small THEN $z$ is Low
2. IF $x$ is Low AND $y$ is Large THEN $z$ is Medium
3. IF $x$ is High AND $y$ is Small THEN $z$ is Medium
4. IF $x$ is High AND $y$ is Large THEN $z$ is High

### Step 1: Fuzzification (Compute Memberships for $x_0 = 5$)

For $A_1$ (Low), peak at $x=3$, right edge at $x=6$:
$$ \mu_{A_1}(5) = \frac{6 - 5}{6 - 3} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 $$

For $A_2$ (High), peak at $x=8$, left edge at $x=5$:
$$ \mu_{A_2}(5) = \frac{5 - 5}{8 - 5} = 0 $$

**Conversion Logic:** The point $x=5$ lies exactly on the left base of $A_2$, so its membership is **0** (boundary condition). It lies on the **descending slope** of $A_1$ between peak (3) and right base (6), so we use the linear descending branch.

### Step 2: Fuzzification (Compute Memberships for $y_0 = 7$)

For $B_1$ (Small), peak at $y=5$, right base at $y=10$:
$$ \mu_{B_1}(7) = \frac{10 - 7}{10 - 5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 $$

For $B_2$ (Large), peak at $y=10$, left base at $y=6$:
$$ \mu_{B_2}(7) = \frac{7 - 6}{10 - 6} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $$

**Conversion Logic:** Point $y=7$ is in the **descending region** of $B_1$ and the **ascending region** of $B_2$.

### Step 3: Compute Firing Strengths (Mamdani Minimum t-norm)

**Rule 1:** $x$ Low AND $y$ Small
$$ \alpha_1 = \min(\mu_{A_1}, \mu_{B_1}) = \min(0.333, 0.6) = 0.333 $$

**Rule 2:** $x$ Low AND $y$ Large
$$ \alpha_2 = \min(\mu_{A_1}, \mu_{B_2}) = \min(0.333, 0.25) = 0.25 $$

**Rule 3:** $x$ High AND $y$ Small
$$ \alpha_3 = \min(\mu_{A_2}, \mu_{B_1}) = \min(0, 0.6) = 0 $$

**Rule 4:** $x$ High AND $y$ Large
$$ \alpha_4 = \min(\mu_{A_2}, \mu_{B_2}) = \min(0, 0.25) = 0 $$

### Step 4: Implication (Singleton Consequent Clipping)

Each singleton $C_i$ at $z = 2, 5, 5, 8$ is **clipped** to height $\alpha_i$:
- $C_1' = \{2/0.333\}$
- $C_2' = \{5/0.25\}$
- $C_3' = \{5/0\}$ (no contribution)
- $C_4' = \{8/0\}$ (no contribution)

### Step 5: Defuzzification (Center of Gravity over Singletons)

For singletons, COG simplifies to weighted average:
$$ z^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_i \cdot z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_i} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{(0.333)(2) + (0.25)(5) + (0)(5) + (0)(8)}{0.333 + 0.25 + 0 + 0} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{0.666 + 1.25 + 0 + 0}{0.583} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{1.916}{0.583} \approx 3.286 $$

**Final Answer:** $z^* \approx \mathbf{3.29}$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Sugeno FIS (Zero-Order)

**Same rules, but consequents are crisp constants instead of fuzzy sets:**
- $C_1 = 2$, $C_2 = 5$, $C_3 = 6$, $C_4 = 9$

Using the **same firing strengths** computed in Section 3.1:

$$ z^* = \frac{\sum w_i z_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{(0.333)(2) + (0.25)(5) + (0)(6) + (0)(9)}{0.333 + 0.25 + 0 + 0} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{0.666 + 1.25}{0.583} = \frac{1.916}{0.583} \approx 3.286 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Coincidence:** The answer is identical in this case because the Sugeno zero-order consequence with weight $w_i$ directly **is** the weighted average, and the Mamdani COG over singletons reduces to the same formula. The two methods differ when the Mamdani consequent is a *non-singleton* fuzzy set.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Sugeno FIS (First-Order)

Suppose Rule $i$ is:
$$ \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_i \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B_i \text{ THEN } z_i = p_i x + q_i y + r_i $$

Given data:
- $x_0 = 5$, $y_0 = 7$
- $\alpha_1 = 0.333$, $\alpha_2 = 0.25$, $\alpha_3 = \alpha_4 = 0$
- $z_1 = 0.1 x + 0.2 y + 1 = 0.5 + 1.4 + 1 = 2.9$
- $z_2 = 0.05 x + 0.3 y + 0.5 = 0.25 + 2.1 + 0.5 = 2.85$

**Weighted average:**
$$ z^* = \frac{\alpha_1 z_1 + \alpha_2 z_2}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{(0.333)(2.9) + (0.25)(2.85)}{0.583} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{0.966 + 0.7125}{0.583} = \frac{1.6785}{0.583} \approx 2.879 $$

**Final Answer:** $z^* \approx \mathbf{2.88}$

---

## 3.4 Derivations: Centroid (COG) Defuzzification for a Triangular Membership Function

Consider a triangular MF with vertices $(a, 0)$, $(b, 1)$, $(c, 0)$ where $a < b < c$.

The membership function is:
$$ \mu(z) = \begin{cases} \frac{z - a}{b - a} & a \le z \le b \\ \frac{c - z}{c - b} & b \le z \le c \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

The COG defuzzification for a single triangle gives:
$$ z^* = \frac{\int_a^c z \cdot \mu(z) \, dz}{\int_a^c \mu(z) \, dz} $$

**Numerator** (split integral):
$$ \int_a^b z \cdot \frac{z-a}{b-a} \, dz + \int_b^c z \cdot \frac{c-z}{c-b} \, dz $$

**First integral** (let $u = z - a$):
$$ \frac{1}{b-a} \int_a^b z(z-a)\,dz = \frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b (z^2 - az)\,dz $$

$$ = \frac{1}{b-a} \left[ \frac{z^3}{3} - \frac{a z^2}{2} \right]_a^b $$

$$ = \frac{1}{b-a} \left[ \left(\frac{b^3}{3} - \frac{ab^2}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{a^3}{3} - \frac{a^3}{2}\right) \right] $$

**Second integral** (by symmetry, similar derivation):
$$ = \frac{1}{c-b} \left[ \frac{c z^2}{2} - \frac{z^3}{3} \right]_b^c $$

**Denominator** (area of triangle):
$$ \int_a^c \mu(z)\,dz = \frac{1}{2}(c - a) \cdot 1 = \frac{c - a}{2} $$

**Closed-form COG for a symmetric triangle** ($a$ and $c$ equidistant from $b$):
$$ \boxed{z^* = \frac{a + 2b + c}{4} = \frac{a+b+c}{4} \text{ (symmetric)} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners love to ask "derive the COG formula for a triangular MF" or "what is the centroid of a symmetric triangle?" The closed-form $z^* = (a+b+c)/4$ for a symmetric triangle is a **favourite 7-mark derivational question**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Choosing the Right Method

| Scenario | Recommended FIS | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Expert knowledge in plain language | **Mamdani** | Linguistic interpretability |
| Real-time embedded control with limited CPU | **Sugeno** | Lower computational cost |
| Training with gradient descent (ANFIS) | **Sugeno** | Differentiable output |
| Adaptive learning of non-linear plant | **Sugeno + NN** | ANFIS synergy |
| Decision support, medical diagnosis | **Mamdani** | Readable rules, transparent reasoning |
| Highly non-linear process control | **Mamdani FLC** | Robust to uncertainty |
| Pattern recognition with labeled data | **NN only** (FIS not suitable) | No need for interpretability |
| Online adaptive inverse control | **NN (recurrent/RBF)** | Real-time weight updates |

---

## 3.6 Stability Analysis: Lyapunov-Based NN Weight Update (Advanced)

For a NN controller applied to a non-linear plant, a **Lyapunov function** can be designed:
$$ V(e, \tilde{W}) = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma} \text{tr}(\tilde{W}^T \tilde{W}) $$

where $e = y_d - y$ is tracking error and $\tilde{W} = W - W^*$ is the weight estimation error.

To ensure $\dot{V} \le 0$ (stability), the weight update law is derived as:
$$ \dot{W} = -\gamma \frac{\partial E}{\partial W} = \gamma e \, \varphi'(x) \, x $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Derive a Lyapunov-based weight update law for an NN controller tracking a non-linear plant." Show the derivation of $V$, take its derivative, apply the chain rule, and conclude with bounded stability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[Crisp Input e and delta e] --> fuzzifyNode[Fuzzification Interface]
    fuzzifyNode --> kbNode[Knowledge Base: Rules and MFs]
    kbNode --> inferNode[Inference Engine]
    inferNode --> defuzzNode[Defuzzification Interface]
    defuzzNode --> plantNode[Controlled Plant]
    plantNode --> outputNode[Crisp Output u]
    outputNode --> fbNode[Feedback Sensor]
    fbNode --> inputNode
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mamdani vs. Sugeno Inference Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Inputs] --> fuzzA[Fuzzification]
    fuzzA --> strengthA[Rule Firing Strength: min or product]
    strengthA --> implA[Implication: min Clipping]
    implA --> aggA[Aggregation: max]
    aggA --> defuzzA[Defuzzification: Centroid Integration]
    defuzzA --> outA[Output Crisp]

    startB[Inputs] --> fuzzB[Fuzzification]
    fuzzB --> strengthB[Rule Firing Strength: product]
    strengthB --> computeB[Compute Consequent Function: constant or linear]
    computeB --> weightB[Weighted Average of Rule Outputs]
    weightB --> outB[Output Crisp]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Neural Network Roles in Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    refNode[Reference yd] --> ctrlNode[Controller]
    ctrlNode --> plantNode[Plant or Process]
    plantNode --> outNode[Output y]
    outNode --> fbNode[Feedback]
    fbNode --> errNode[Error e = yd minus y]
    errNode --> ctrlNode

    subgraph "NN Roles"
        nn1[NN as System Identifier: learns plant forward dynamics]
        nn2[NN as Controller: learns plant inverse dynamics]
        nn3[NN as Adaptive Gain: adjusts Kp, Ki, Kd online]
        nn4[NN as State Estimator: predicts unmeasurable states]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    l1[Layer 1: Input Fuzzification: mu_A and mu_B] --> l2[Layer 2: Rule Firing Strength: w = mu_A times mu_B]
    l2 --> l3[Layer 3: Normalized Firing: w_normalized = w / sum w]
    l3 --> l4[Layer 4: Consequent Function: w times p x + q y + r]
    l4 --> l5[Layer 5: Summation: y = sum w_i f_i]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** ANFIS is the **fusion** of Sugeno FIS (5 layers) with a backprop-trained neural network. The **consequent parameters** ($p, q, r$) are learned by **least-squares**, and the **premise parameters** (MF shape) are learned by **gradient descent**. This is a popular 14-mark question in KTU exams.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Inverted Pendulum Control using NN/FLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    angleNode[Angle theta] --> fbNode2[Feedback]
    posNode[Cart Position x] --> fbNode2
    fbNode2 --> flcNode2[FLC or NN Controller]
    flcNode2 --> forceNode[Force F on Cart]
    forceNode --> pendulumNode[Inverted Pendulum Plant]
    pendulumNode --> angleNode
    pendulumNode --> posNode
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Direct Inverse Control Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refNode2[Reference yd] --> invNN[Trained Inverse NN Model]
    invNN --> plantCtrl[Plant]
    plantCtrl --> outY2[Output y]
    outY2 --> errComp2[Error Comparator]
    refNode2 --> errComp2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Mamdani and Sugeno fuzzy inference systems. List any four differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mamdani FIS | Sugeno FIS |
|---|---|---|
| Consequent | Linguistic (fuzzy set) | Mathematical (constant or linear) |
| Defuzzification | Integration (COG, BOA, MoM) | Weighted average (no integration) |
| Computational cost | Higher | Lower |
| Interpretability | High (English-like rules) | Low (math expressions) |
| Suitability for learning | Limited | Excellent (used in ANFIS) |

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)? List its four main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A Fuzzy Logic Controller is a real-time expert system that uses fuzzy logic and linguistic IF–THEN rules to compute a control signal for non-linear or uncertain plants.

The four main components are:
1. **Fuzzification Interface** — converts crisp input to fuzzy membership.
2. **Knowledge Base** — stores rule base and membership function database.
3. **Inference Engine** — performs fuzzy reasoning using t-norms and implications.
4. **Defuzzification Interface** — converts fuzzy output back to a crisp control value.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Confusing **Sugeno zero-order** ($z = k$) with **Mamdani singletons** — they look similar but differ in defuzzification framework.
> 2. Forgetting to **normalize** the firing strengths in Sugeno (denominator $\sum w_i$ is mandatory).
> 3. Using **product t-norm** for Mamdani when the question specifies **min t-norm** — always read the question carefully.
> 4. Omitting the **conversion logic** ("why this step") in derivations — KTU examiners reward explanation as much as the math.
> 5. For ANFIS questions, students often mislabel Layer 3 vs Layer 4 — remember Layer 3 is **normalization**, Layer 4 is the **consequent function**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System (FIS)**. Illustrate with a suitable block diagram and explain each of the four steps: fuzzification, rule evaluation, implication, and defuzzification. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A two-input single-output Mamdani system has the following rules. Input $x_0 = 4$, $y_0 = 6$. Memberships: $\mu_{A_1}(4) = 0.7$, $\mu_{A_2}(4) = 0.2$, $\mu_{B_1}(6) = 0.4$, $\mu_{B_2}(6) = 0.8$. Compute the crisp output $z^*$ using the COG method (assume singleton consequents $z_1 = 2, z_2 = 4, z_3 = 5, z_4 = 7$). **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution (a):**
The **Mamdani FIS** (Ebrahim Mamdani, 1975) is a fuzzy rule-based system in which both antecedents and consequents of rules are **linguistic fuzzy sets**.

**Step 1 – Fuzzification:** Convert crisp $x_0, y_0$ into fuzzy memberships:
$$ \mu_{A_i}(x_0), \mu_{B_j}(y_0) \in [0,1] $$

**Step 2 – Rule Evaluation (Firing Strength):** For rule $k$:
$$ \alpha_k = \mu_{A_k}(x_0) \star \mu_{B_k}(y_0) $$
where $\star$ is a t-norm (usually **min** for AND).

**Step 3 – Implication:** Clip the consequent fuzzy set at height $\alpha_k$:
$$ \mu_{C_k'}(z) = \min(\alpha_k, \mu_{C_k}(z)) $$

**Step 4 – Aggregation and Defuzzification (COG):** Combine all clipped outputs via max, then take centroid:
$$ z^* = \frac{\int z \cdot \mu_{C_{\text{agg}}}(z)\,dz}{\int \mu_{C_{\text{agg}}}(z)\,dz} $$

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Rule Firing Strengths (min t-norm):**
- $w_1 = \min(0.7, 0.4) = 0.4$ → $z_1 = 2$
- $w_2 = \min(0.7, 0.8) = 0.7$ → $z_2 = 4$
- $w_3 = \min(0.2, 0.4) = 0.2$ → $z_3 = 5$
- $w_4 = \min(0.2, 0.8) = 0.2$ → $z_4 = 7$

**Step 2: COG for Singletons (weighted average):**
$$ z^* = \frac{(0.4)(2) + (0.7)(4) + (0.2)(5) + (0.2)(7)}{0.4 + 0.7 + 0.2 + 0.2} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{0.8 + 2.8 + 1.0 + 1.4}{1.5} = \frac{6.0}{1.5} = 4.0 $$

**Final Answer:** $z^* = \mathbf{4.0}$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Sugeno (TSK) Fuzzy Inference System**. Compare it with the Mamdani FIS. Discuss its advantages in adaptive control applications. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A Sugeno FIS has two rules with the following consequents:
- Rule 1: IF $x$ is $A_1$ AND $y$ is $B_1$ THEN $z_1 = 0.5x + 0.3y + 1$
- Rule 2: IF $x$ is $A_2$ AND $y$ is $B_2$ THEN $z_2 = 0.2x - 0.1y + 4$

For $x_0 = 6, y_0 = 8$, the firing strengths are $w_1 = 0.6$ and $w_2 = 0.3$. Compute $z^*$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution (a):**
The **Sugeno FIS** (Takagi, Sugeno, Kang, 1985) uses a **fuzzy antecedent** and a **crisp polynomial consequent**. The general rule form is:
$$ R_i: \text{ IF } x \text{ is } A_i \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B_i \text{ THEN } z_i = a_i x + b_i y + c_i $$

**Firing Strength:**
$$ w_i = \mu_{A_i}(x) \cdot \mu_{B_i}(y) \quad \text{(product t-norm)} $$

**Defuzzification (Weighted Average):**
$$ z^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{R} w_i \cdot z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{R} w_i} $$

**Comparison with Mamdani (already shown in table 2.3)**, with key advantages:
- **Computational efficiency** (no integration).
- **Smooth and continuous output surface** (linear interpolation between rules).
- **Differentiable** — enables gradient-based learning.
- **Compatible with ANFIS** for adaptive tuning.
- **Mathematically tractable** for stability analysis.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute consequent values at $x_0 = 6, y_0 = 8$:**
$$ z_1 = 0.5(6) + 0.3(8) + 1 = 3 + 2.4 + 1 = 6.4 $$
$$ z_2 = 0.2(6) - 0.1(8) + 4 = 1.2 - 0.8 + 4 = 4.4 $$

**Step 2: Weighted average defuzzification:**
$$ z^* = \frac{w_1 z_1 + w_2 z_2}{w_1 + w_2} = \frac{(0.6)(6.4) + (0.3)(4.4)}{0.6 + 0.3} $$

$$ z^* = \frac{3.84 + 1.32}{0.9} = \frac{5.16}{0.9} = 5.733 $$

**Final Answer:** $z^* \approx \mathbf{5.73}$

---

## 5.3 Part B Alternative: 14-Mark Question on NN in Control

### Question C (Choice 3): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Anticipated Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **roles of Neural Networks in Control Systems**. Explain with diagrams: (i) NN as system identifier, (ii) NN as controller (direct inverse control), and (iii) NN in Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a 2-2-1 feedforward neural network (2 inputs, 2 hidden, 1 output) used as a controller, derive the **backpropagation weight update equations** for the hidden-to-output and input-to-hidden layers. Use sigmoid activation $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution (a) – Summary Outline:**
- **NN as System Identifier:** Learns $\hat{f} \approx f_{\text{plant}}$ from I/O data; used for prediction and IMC.
- **NN as Controller (Direct Inverse):** Learns plant inverse; placed in series with plant.
- **NN in MRAC:** Adjusts weights so plant output tracks a reference model; uses Lyapunov-based weight update.
- **NN in NN-MPC:** Predicts future outputs; optimizer minimizes cost function.
- **NN as Adaptive Gain:** Tunes PID gains in real-time.

(Refer to Section 2.6 for detailed explanations.)

**Model Solution (b) – Derivation Outline:**

Network equations:
$$ h_j = \sigma\left(\sum_{i=1}^{2} w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j^{(1)}\right), \quad j = 1, 2 $$

$$ y = \sigma\left(\sum_{j=1}^{2} w_{j}^{(2)} h_j + b^{(2)}\right) $$

Error:
$$ E = \frac{1}{2}(y_d - y)^2 $$

**Output layer gradient:**
$$ \delta_o = (y_d - y) \cdot y (1 - y) $$
$$ w_j^{(2)} \leftarrow w_j^{(2)} + \eta \delta_o h_j $$

**Hidden layer gradient (chain rule):**
$$ \delta_j = \delta_o \cdot w_j^{(2)} \cdot h_j(1 - h_j) $$
$$ w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} + \eta \delta_j x_i $$

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Soft Computing):**

- **Mamdani FIS** uses fuzzy set consequents → needs **integration-based** defuzzification (COG, BOA, MoM).
- **Sugeno FIS** uses constant/linear consequents → uses **weighted average** defuzzification (no integration).
- **Sugeno = computationally cheaper + differentiable + ANFIS-compatible**.
- **FLC** has 4 modules: Fuzzification, Knowledge Base, Inference Engine, Defuzzification.
- **Standard FLC inputs:** Error ($e$) and Change in Error ($\Delta e$).
- **Common rule format:** 7 linguistic labels (NB, NM, NS, ZE, PS, PM, PB).
- **First commercial FLC application:** Mitsubishi air conditioner (1987).
- **ANN in Control** roles: System Identifier, Controller, MRAC, NN-MPC, Adaptive Gain.
- **Direct Inverse Control** (Widrow) is the simplest NN-based control scheme.
